BharSt_1.1: Salutation to the boundless light of consciousness that is beyond time and space and known through direct inner realization.
BharSt_1.2: Good sayings suffer everywhere: the learned are often jealous, the powerful are proud, and the uncomprehending cannot receive them.
BharSt_1.3: An ignorant person is easy to guide, and a truly learned person easier still; the half-taught egoist is hardest of all.
BharSt_1.4: One might dare impossible physical feats, but it is still easier than satisfying the mind of a stubborn fool.
BharSt_1.5: Oil from sand, water from a mirage, or horns on a rabbit may be imagined; pleasing an obstinate fool may not.
BharSt_1.6: Trying to reform the wicked with sweet instruction is like binding an elephant with lotus fibre or sweetening the sea with honey.

BharSt_1.7.en: Silence is the one dependable covering for ignorance; among the truly learned, it is the fool's best ornament.
BharSt_1.7.te: అజ్ఞానానికి నమ్మదగిన ఒకే ఒక ఆవరణం మౌనం; నిజమైన పండితుల మధ్య అది మూర్ఖునికి ఉత్తమ అలంకారం.

BharSt_1.8: When I knew little, pride made me feel all-knowing; learning from the wise cured that fever.
BharSt_1.9: The small-minded cling to what is worthless, just as a dog gnaws a foul bone without noticing anything greater nearby.
BharSt_1.10: When discernment fails, downfall multiplies, just as the Ganges descends step by step from heaven to earth and below.
BharSt_1.11: There is a remedy for fire, heat, beasts, disease, and poison; but there is no medicine for folly.
BharSt_1.12: A person without literature, music, or the arts is like an animal in human shape, lacking only tail and horns.
BharSt_1.13: Those without learning, discipline, generosity, character, or dharma merely burden the earth in human form.
BharSt_1.14: Better to wander in mountain forests than to live in heavenly comfort among fools.
BharSt_1.15: If poets and teachers live in poverty under a ruler, the disgrace belongs to the ruler, not to the learned.
BharSt_1.16: Learning is a hidden treasure that grows by being shared and cannot be stolen or exhausted.
BharSt_1.17: Do not slight those who have realized truth; wealth cannot bind them any more than a lotus fibre can restrain an elephant.
BharSt_1.18: Fate may deprive the swan of its lotus-lake, but it cannot take away its power to separate milk from water.
BharSt_1.19: Jewels and perfumes do not truly adorn a person; refined speech does.
BharSt_1.20: Learning is hidden wealth, a companion abroad, a source of joy and honor, and the true mark of a human being.
BharSt_1.21: When inner virtues are present, outer supports become unnecessary; patience, truth, modesty, and poetry outweigh armour, penance, and power.

BharSt_1.22.en: Skill in life means fitting conduct to each relation: kindness here, firmness there, courage, restraint, and tact in due measure.
BharSt_1.22.te: జీవిత నైపుణ్యం అంటే ప్రతి సంబంధానికి తగిన ప్రవర్తనను తెలిసి ఉండటం: ఎక్కడ దయ, ఎక్కడ దృఢత్వం, ఎక్కడ శౌర్యం, ఎక్కడ సంయమనం, ఎక్కడ చాతుర్యం చూపాలో తెలుసుకోవడం.

BharSt_1.23.en: Good company removes dullness, plants truth in speech, lifts self-respect, purifies the heart, and spreads good name everywhere.
BharSt_1.23.te: సత్సంగతి మాంద్యాన్ని తొలగిస్తుంది, మాటల్లో నిజాన్ని నాటుతుంది, ఆత్మగౌరవాన్ని పెంచుతుంది, హృదయాన్ని శుద్ధి చేస్తుంది, మంచి పేరును అన్ని వైపులా వ్యాపింపజేస్తుంది.

BharSt_1.24: Blessed are the accomplished poets whose living body of fame escapes the fear of decay and death.
BharSt_1.25: A good child, faithful spouse, sincere friend, calm mind, stable means, and clear learning come only by deep merit.
BharSt_1.26: Nonviolence, honesty, self-restraint, timely giving, humility, compassion, and freedom from craving mark the path of true welfare.
BharSt_1.27: The noble do not abandon what they begin out of fear of obstacles; they persist through them.
BharSt_1.28: The difficult vow of the good is to keep honor, right conduct, and steadiness even under strain.
BharSt_1.29: Even starving and ruined, the lion still seeks prey worthy of its nature; the great do not stoop beneath themselves.
BharSt_1.30: Every being seeks a result in keeping with its nature; the weak are satisfied with scraps, the strong with worthy aims.

BharSt_1.31.en: A dog fawns for food, but an elephant keeps its dignity and eats only when properly invited.
BharSt_1.31.te: ఆహారం కోసం కుక్క చాటువాడుతుంది; గజం మాత్రం తన గౌరవాన్ని నిలబెట్టి, తగిన ఆహ్వానం వచ్చిన తర్వాతే తింటుంది.

BharSt_1.32: A person is truly born when their birth raises the dignity of the lineage.
BharSt_1.33: High-minded people have two ways only: to stand at the top of the world or to perish unnoticed in the forest.
BharSt_1.34: Rahu attacks only the sun and moon; real power chooses worthy targets, not easy ones.
BharSt_1.35: The truly great carry great burdens with astonishing ease, as the cosmic beings bear world upon world.
BharSt_1.36: Better to be shattered nobly than to flee ignobly when those to whom one owes loyalty are in distress.
BharSt_1.37: A lion cub still leaps at elephants; brilliance depends on nature, not merely on age.
BharSt_1.38: Without wealth, birth, virtues, family, and heroism are often treated as grass; material standing distorts worldly judgment.
BharSt_1.39: Earn wealth, says the world, for without it many other excellences are ignored; the line is bitter and diagnostic, not celebratory.
BharSt_1.40: The person remains the same in body and mind, yet loss of wealth instantly changes how the world sees them.
BharSt_1.41: In the world's eyes, wealth makes one seem noble, learned, eloquent, and handsome; gold gathers borrowed virtues around it.
BharSt_1.42: Kings fall through bad counsel, ascetics through entanglement, families through bad conduct, and wealth through waste and neglect.

BharSt_1.43.en: Wealth has only three destinations: giving, enjoying, or losing; if one neither gives nor enjoys, loss remains.
BharSt_1.43.te: సంపదకు మూడు గమ్యాలే ఉన్నాయి: దానం, అనుభవం, లేక నాశనం. ఇవ్వకపోతే, అనుభవించకపోతే, చివరికి మిగిలేది నష్టమే.

BharSt_1.44: Things often become more beautiful after hardship: jewels, rivers, the moon, and even people who have spent themselves for others.
BharSt_1.45: The same object seems great or small depending on the condition of the one who holds it.

BharSt_1.46.en: If a ruler would milk the earth, let him first nourish the people as a calf is nourished; then the land yields abundance.
BharSt_1.46.te: భూమి నుంచి ఫలితాన్ని పొందాలనుకునే రాజు ముందుగా ప్రజలను దూడను పోషించినట్టు పోషించాలి; అప్పుడు నేల సమృద్ధిగా ఫలిస్తుంది.

BharSt_1.47: Statecraft is many-formed and unstable, shifting between truth and falsehood, gentleness and harshness, giving and grasping.
BharSt_1.48: If a ruler does not command, protect, honor wisdom, give, enjoy rightly, and safeguard friends, service to that ruler is empty.
BharSt_1.49: Each person receives only what is written for them; a pot draws the same measure whether dipped in a well or the sea.
BharSt_1.50: Do not lower yourself before every cloud; among many, only some truly rain.
BharSt_1.51: Ask carefully and sparingly, for many loud promises are empty; not every cloud gives water.
BharSt_1.52: Cruelty, groundless quarrel, lust for others' wealth or partners, and envy of the good are natural to the wicked.
BharSt_1.53: Avoid the wicked even when adorned with learning; a jeweled serpent is still a serpent.
BharSt_1.54: The wicked recast every virtue as a fault: silence as stupidity, bravery as cruelty, purity as hypocrisy, steadiness as weakness.
BharSt_1.55: If greed is present, what more vice is needed? If truth and purity are present, what more ritual is required?
BharSt_1.56: Seven things trouble the mind: faded beauty, empty waters, dullness in a handsome face, greed in a master, distress in the good, and honor for the wicked.
BharSt_1.57: Kings of violent anger have no true intimates; fire burns even the priest who feeds it.
BharSt_1.58: Service is exceedingly difficult: silence is taken for dumbness, pleasant speech for foolish chatter, patience for fear, distance for coldness.
BharSt_1.59: It is painful to watch a base person gain wealth by fate and then roam unchecked in contempt of virtue.
BharSt_1.60: The friendship of the wicked begins grandly and soon thins away; the friendship of the good grows slowly and deepens.
BharSt_1.61: Deer, fish, and good people live simply, yet hunters, fishers, and slanderers pursue them without cause.
BharSt_1.62: Long for good company, delight in others' virtues, honor teachers, love learning, practice self-restraint, and shun the wicked.
BharSt_1.63: In the great, courage in trouble, restraint in prosperity, eloquence, valor, love of fame, and devotion to learning arise naturally.
BharSt_1.64: The conduct of the good is sharp as a sword's edge: giving quietly, honoring guests, hiding one's own goodness, and remaining humble in prosperity.

BharSt_1.65.en: The true ornaments of the great are generosity, reverence, truthful speech, courage, clean conduct, and learning.
BharSt_1.65.te: మహనీయుల నిజమైన ఆభరణాలు దానం, వినయం, సత్యవచనం, ధైర్యం, శుద్ధమైన ప్రవర్తన, విద్య.

BharSt_1.66: The hearts of the great are lotus-soft in prosperity and mountain-hard in adversity.
BharSt_1.67: A drop becomes nothing on hot iron, shines on a lotus leaf, and becomes a pearl in the oyster; association shapes destiny.
BharSt_1.68: A worthy child, faithful spouse, and steadfast friend are gained only through merit.
BharSt_1.69: For one whose mind is simple and steadfast, one God, one friend, one home, and one beloved are enough.
BharSt_1.70: The noble bend through humility, praise others, pursue their own ends without harming others, and answer harshness with patience.
BharSt_1.71: Trees bend when laden with fruit, clouds when heavy with rain; so too the truly helpful become more humble when prosperous.
BharSt_1.72: The ears are adorned by learning, the hands by giving, and the body by service to others, not by ornaments.
BharSt_1.73: A true friend restrains from wrong, guides toward good, keeps secrets, reveals virtues, stands firm in trouble, and helps at the right time.
BharSt_1.74: The sun, moon, and rain-cloud help the world unasked; so do the truly generous.

BharSt_1.75.en: Some abandon self-interest for others, some help where no conflict arises, some injure others for selfish gain, and some even do so for nothing.
BharSt_1.75.te: కొంతమంది పరుల కోసం తమ స్వార్థాన్ని విడిచిపెడతారు; ఇంకొందరు తమకు నష్టం లేకపోతే సహాయం చేస్తారు; మరికొందరు తమ ప్రయోజనం కోసం పరులకు హాని చేస్తారు; మరి కొందరు ఎలాంటి ప్రయోజనం లేకుండానే హాని చేస్తారు.

BharSt_1.76: The friendship of the good is like milk and water: each gives itself for the other, and returns to harmony together.
BharSt_1.77: The ocean bears Vishnu, mountains, and fire within itself; the great have vast capacity for burden.
BharSt_1.78: Cut craving, cultivate patience, abandon pride, speak truth, follow the good, honor the wise, guard your reputation, and show compassion.
BharSt_1.79: How few are those whose mind, speech, and body are filled with nectar-like goodness and who rejoice in magnifying the virtues of others.
BharSt_1.80: What use is a mountain of gold if it changes nothing? Blessed is the place whose influence turns even bitter trees fragrant.
BharSt_1.81: The steadfast do not stop until the aim is fully won, just as the gods did not rest before securing nectar.
BharSt_1.82: A person of resolve does not count changing conditions as pleasure or pain while intent on the work.
BharSt_1.83: Goodness adorns prosperity, restraint adorns power, humility adorns learning, truth adorns dharma; character adorns all.
BharSt_1.84: The steady do not depart from the just path whether praised or blamed, wealthy or poor, dying now or much later.
BharSt_1.85: Fate alone seems to raise and ruin: a starving snake is saved by the very rat that falls into its basket.
BharSt_1.86: Laziness is the great enemy within the body; effort is the truest ally and does not fail the one who acts.
BharSt_1.87: Trees sprout again and the moon waxes again; remembering this, the good do not collapse in grief.
BharSt_1.88: Even Indra with all his advantages was defeated; such is the terrifying power of fate over human effort.
BharSt_1.89: Results follow action, and judgment itself is shaped by action; therefore act only after deep reflection.
BharSt_1.90: Misfortune follows the unlucky wherever they run, as the palm fruit struck the traveler who sought its shade.
BharSt_1.91: Seeing the wealthy afflicted, the brilliant poor, and the mighty bound, one cannot help feeling the force of fate.
BharSt_1.92: The Creator makes a jewel of a human being, and then spoils it with folly; what a tragic contradiction.
BharSt_1.93: If no leaves grow on the barren tree, or the owl cannot see the sun, the fault is not in spring or sunlight; destiny has its own script.
BharSt_1.94: If even gods and fate merely dispense the fruits of action, then action itself deserves deepest reverence.
BharSt_1.95: Action has compelled Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva, and the sun themselves; therefore bow to action.
BharSt_1.96: Form, birth, character, learning, and service do not bear fruit by themselves; accumulated merit ripens in its own time.
BharSt_1.97: In forest, war, fire, ocean, mountain, sleep, madness, and peril, past merit protects.
BharSt_1.98: Noble action can transform the wicked, the foolish, and even poison itself; therefore devote yourself to good action.
BharSt_1.99: Before doing any deed, whether attractive or not, the wise examine its outcome; rash action leaves a heart-burning aftereffect.
BharSt_1.100: To gain this human birth and spend it on petty ends is like using jewels, gold, sandalwood, and camphor for trivial grain.
BharSt_1.101: One may strive in every way, but what is not destined does not occur, and what is destined does not vanish.
BharSt_1.102: For the one rich in past merit, the world turns friendly, habitable, and full of treasure.
BharSt_1.103: True gain is noble company, true loss is missing the right time, true wealth is learning, and true heroism is mastery of the senses.
BharSt_1.104: The earth is adorned here and there by those who speak kindly, stay content with their own, and refrain from slandering others.
BharSt_1.105: Even when insulted, true courage does not lose its upward flame, just as fire's flame does not turn downward.
BharSt_1.106: Who is not pierced by desire, burned by anger, or dragged by greed conquers the three worlds within.
BharSt_1.107: One heroic force can encompass the world, just as the sun alone floods it with light.
BharSt_1.108: For the person whose character fully flowers, fire becomes water, poison becomes nectar, and danger loses its sting.
BharSt_1.109: The truly radiant may give up life itself, but not the vow of truth once they have pledged their word.
